one_line_takeaway: A top-down survey that unifies backdoor attacks, weight attacks, and adversarial examples under one S/C/I optimization formulation mapped onto the five-stage ML life-cycle, giving a shared taxonomy for otherwise-siloed AML attack research - but it is a conceptual/organizational contribution with no experiments of its own.
key_finding: Backdoor attacks, weight attacks, and inference-time adversarial examples - developed almost independently - are all instances of one optimization template differing only in which of the S, C, I terms and which of the benign/adversarial weights (w_0, w_eps) appear. Backdoor attacks involve no benign model input (only w_eps), weight attacks involve both w_0 and w_eps with a hard benign-consistency constraint L_CB on the original model, and adversarial examples involve only the benign model w_0. This shared structure, plus the life-cycle placement, is presented as the unifying lens that lets a researcher in one branch see its relationship to the others.
core_contribution: This is an attack-only survey of adversarial machine learning (AML) whose OWN contribution is conceptual, not empirical: (1) a general definition of AML built on three conditions - stealthiness S, benign consistency C, and adversarial inconsistency I - and (2) a single unified mathematical framework, arg min over (x_eps, w_eps) of S + C + I, from which the authors show that three historically siloed attack paradigms can each be recovered by specializing the terms. The three paradigms are organized along a five-stage ML system life-cycle (pre-training, training, post-training, deployment, inference): backdoor attacks (pre-training/training + inference activation), weight attacks (post-training/deployment + inference), and adversarial examples (inference only). Using this framework the authors build a full taxonomy that categorizes and reviews representative methods for each paradigm, and argue the unification exposes intrinsic connections and differences across branches that isolated sub-field surveys miss. Note: the individual attack methods catalogued (e.g. BadDiffusion, TrojDiff, VillanDiffusion, PPT, BadPrompt, AutoDAN, TrojLLM, Rowhammer/bit-flip weight attacks) are CITED prior work being organized, not this paper's own techniques. The paper's contribution is the taxonomy and unifying formulation, plus a continuously-updated companion website (adversarial-ml.com).
threat_model: The survey does not adopt one threat model but taxonomizes adversaries by their access at each life-cycle stage. Pre-training: attacker can only manipulate part of an untrusted/third-party training dataset (poisoned-data-injection backdoors), no control of training. Training: attacker controls the training procedure/loss (training-controllable backdoors). Post-training: attacker can directly modify model weights in continuous space (parameter-modification weight attacks). Deployment: attacker flips bits of the deployed model in hardware memory (bit-flip weight attacks, relying on physical fault injection such as Rowhammer or laser). Inference: attacker perturbs a benign input under a stealthiness constraint to flip the benign model's prediction (adversarial examples), and is also where backdoor/weight attacks are activated. The unifying goal across all: keep behavior benign on clean data while producing a target/incorrect output on the adversarial trigger/input, under a stealthiness constraint on the sample or weight perturbation.
limitations: Stated or evident from the paper itself: (1) Scope is deliberately attack-only; defenses are deferred to a separate companion survey [249]. (2) The unified framework is primarily instantiated on the image-classification task; other domains (diffusion, LLMs) are reviewed narratively rather than folded rigorously into the formulation. (3) As a survey it contributes no empirical validation - the framework's usefulness is argued, not measured, and there is no quantitative comparison of the catalogued methods. (4) The authors themselves note weight attacks remain largely theoretical: to their knowledge no successful weight attack against a real-world intelligent system exists, because bit-flip attacks depend on specialized physical fault-injection expertise. (5) The discussion of 'future trends' (e.g. invisible/dynamic/sample-specific triggers, multi-target backdoors) is the authors' speculation, not established result.
